data.title: Advanced Drainage Systems Is Doing Great, But That Doesn't Mean Gains Deserve To Flow
data.description:
The last few months have been really good time for shareholders of Advanced Drainage Systems (WMS). Since I reaffirmed the company as a ‘hold’ candidate back in September of 2025, the stock has shot up 20.9%. That is significantly greater than the 5.3% rise that the S&P 500 experienced. This comes at a time when revenue, profits, and cash flows, are all rising. What's more, management seems optimistic about the future, and the company recently completed a sizable acquisition that should push revenue and some cash flow metrics even higher. As great as the company is from an operational standpoint, however, it's not really easy to get behind at the moment. Unfortunately, the stock looks pretty solidly fairly valued, both on an absolute basis and relative to companies that have similarities to it. As as much as I would love to turn bullish on it right now, I don't see that being a realistic and fruitful path.

Solid results and big moves

Just the other day, on February 5th, the management team at Advanced Drainage Systems announced financial results for the third quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Revenue for the quarter totaled $693.4 million. Although this was up only a hair compared to the $690.5 million the company reported a year earlier, it did exceed what analysts anticipated to the tune of $9 million. This was not the only improvement that the company saw. Earnings per share popped from $1.04 to $1.19. In fact, the earnings reported by the company ended up being $0.10 greater than what had been anticipated. This meant a rise in net profits from $81.2 million to $93.6 million. Operating cash flow for the business expanded from $190 million to $269.3 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get an increase from $143.5 million to $164.5 million. And finally, EBITDA grew from $191.5 million to $209.2 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial performance for the first nine months of 2026 compared to the same time of 2025. As was the case with the third quarter on its own, revenue, profits, and cash flows, increased for the business. This is part of a pretty consistent trend for the company. However, it is worth noting that not all parts of the company are equal.

During the most recent quarter, two of the three operating segments for the business actually reported declining revenue. The larger of these is its Pipe segment, which is responsible for around 47.1% to the company’s overall sales. Year over year, revenue actually dropped 3.8% from $339.6 million to $326.7 million. This drop, according to the company, can be attributed to lower sales volumes in the residential and infrastructure end markets. To be honest with you, the weakness in the residential side is not shocking to me in the least bit. I have long been talking about weakness in the residential market. The most recent data that we have regarding housing was published in early January and covers October of last year. Housing completions were down 15.3% year over year. Housing starts had dropped 7.8%. Building permits were down 1.1%. Non-residential construction activity ended up dropping about 0.9% year over year for the month of October. And that doesn't even factor in public construction. However, that did see a slight increase from $513.4 billion to $524 billion.

Even though this was the big pain point for the company, the other segment that saw a decline was the International segment. Revenue dipped 2.5% from $50.4 million to $49.1 million. Management did not really reveal much here. But given the weakness that other countries are experiencing when it comes to inflationary pressures and a broader economic malaise that has formed, this doesn't surprise me all that much. The good news is that the other operating segments for the company made up for this.

To a lesser extent, the Infiltrator segment did well, with revenue climbing 1.9% from $150 million to $152.9 million. This, according to the company, was the result of higher demand in the residential market as the need for tanks and treatment products expanded. But even more impressive ended up being the Allied Products & Other segment. Revenue jumped an impressive 9.4% from $150.5 million to $160.7 million. Despite weakness in the residential market and elsewhere, this segment benefited from market share gains that the company was able to capture, benefits from the introduction of new products, and programs dedicated to servicing customers and boosting overall distribution. Examples of key products that did well here ended up being its StormTech storage chambers and its Nyloplast capture structures. Water quality products also did well. And all of this was because of new product introductions at the company achieved in the prior 12 months. It also helps that the firm was aided by more favorable multifamily construction activity.

When it comes to 2026 as a whole, management said that revenue should come in at between $2.99 billion and $3.04 billion. For context, in 2025, revenue was $2.90 billion. From this revenue, the company expects EBITDA to range between $930 million and $960 million. Last year, it stood at $889.2 million. In all likelihood, hitting the midpoint of expectations would mean net profits of about $475.1 million and adjusted operating cash flow of $744 million. Both of these represent increases over what the company achieved in 2025.

Normally, I would go on to value the company on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar firms. But we do need to address the 800 pound elephant in the room. Last year, management announced that they had agreed to acquire National Diversified Sales, which is a water management business owned by Norma Group SE. I absolutely love anything that has to do with the water industry because of my own views regarding water scarcity and how important an issue it will be in the decades to come. Management views this as a highly complementary transaction because of its focus on the residential water management, access box, and irrigation solutions markets. But this is not a small purchase. Management agreed to pay $1 billion for the business. But net of tax benefits, that price is a bit lower at $875 million.

All combined, this newly acquired business generates revenue of around $313 million. About 55% of those sales come from stormwater management activities. Landscape irrigation is responsible for 30%. And the remaining 15% involves flow management services. The vast majority of the company's revenue comes from the Americas. But about 10% of sales come from the Asia Pacific region and EMEA (Europe, the Middle East, and Africa). The overall revenue acquired should bring in annual EBITDA of around $62 million. But if everything goes according to plan, within three years of the completion of the transaction, management expects to capture about $25 million in run rate synergies.

In the table above, you can see calculations that I've performed to arrive at the impact to net profits and operating cash flow that should come from this purchase. I did have to make assumptions in this case, such as those involving tax rates, depreciation, and overall interest expense associated with the rise in net debt. But I believe that these assumptions are all reasonable. Without synergies, we are looking at a slight decline in overall net profits. Cash flows should rise slightly. But with synergies, the picture does look a bit more favorable.

In the chart above, you can see how Advanced Drainage Systems is valued under a few different scenarios. The first scenario looks at how the business is valued using projections for 2026 and results from 2025 without this purchase factored in. The second scenario looks at the purchase being completed, but without any synergies captured. And the third scenario looks at the purchase having been completed with synergies fully achieved. The overall trading multiples don't change drastically. At the end of the day, this means that the picture is relatively low risk for the business. And the fact that the firm does look more attractive under the assumption that synergies are completed means that we are looking at a favorable risk to reward opportunity here.

As part of my analysis, I then compared Advanced Drainage Systems to five other similar enterprises. In addition to the stock looking pretty much fairly valued on an absolute basis, it also looks fairly valued compared to these other enterprises as well. Across the board, two of the five competitors are cheaper than it is, which essentially puts it around the middle of the group in terms of valuation.

Takeaway

As much as I want to be bullish about Advanced Drainage Systems right now, I can't. Even with this exciting acquisition that was just completed, and even with management forecasting rosy results for 2026 compared to 2025, the picture looks decent, but not great. As things stand, the best thing I can do is to maintain my ‘hold’ rating on the stock.

data.title: Inflection Points: Earning Proof Points
data.description:
The last month felt like a year. Halfway through the first earnings season of 2026, and markets have already experienced two volatility spikes.1 The first followed the nomination of Kevin Warsh as Federal Reserve chair and hotter-than-expected producer inflation. The second emerged as mega-cap doubled down on AI investment, stoking fears that firms may struggle to deliver the expected growth and profitability. That concern is likely overdone, as AI integration at the enterprise and consumer agentic levels remains in the early innings.2

Good news is that the breadth of companies participating in the productivity boom is improving and delivering results. Large cap companies have consistently beaten expectations this earnings season and generally revised guidance higher, breaking from historical norms.3 Parsing the data to contextualize recent events helps explain the market rotation and hints at themes to consider. It also provides reassurance that the backdrop remains favorable for risk assets, including popular themes like AI and emerging areas such as materials tied to energy and infrastructure.

A Fundamentals-Driven Market

At the start of 2026, rate markets were pricing in two cuts for the year, but expectations for a second cut have declined.4 The Warsh nomination coinciding with a bout of equity volatility reopened the question whether this market is fundamentals- or rates-driven. The market’s initial read labeled Warsh as a hawk less likely to lower rates in the near term, but we believe that might be too simplistic of a view.5

The hawk–dove narrative misses the broader point: Warsh is likely to take a holistic view on economic growth. For years, the former Fed governor has advocated for policies that would promote economic growth through investment and productivity.6 Lower rates could help accelerate investment, suggesting the probability of two or three cuts remains reasonably high. That said, Warsh would likely look to offset the inflationary pressure from rate cuts through balance sheet reduction, effectively unraveling quantitative easing (QE) policies. He has been critical of QE in the past, noting that the Fed’s intervention impedes market-determined asset prices and investment.7

In theory, lower rates combined with a reduced Fed presence in markets would be a pro-growth mix. Of course, reducing the $6.6 trillion balance sheet is easier said than done and could drive rates higher as the supply of securities increases.8 Deregulation of the financial system, and specifically lower capital requirements for banks, could free up capacity to absorb the supply and minimize the impact on rates.

Despite the volatility around Warsh’s nomination and the perceived rate impact, we view this market as fundamentals-driven. With the large-cap cost of capital still relatively high, lower rates can lift valuations, but that may just be the icing on the cake.9 Equity markets advanced on strong fundamentals in 2025, and we believe that trend is likely to persist.

In 2025, all S&P 500 price gains were realized during the 16 weeks of earnings season, while returns for the remaining 36 weeks were negative.10 Investors responded to positive earnings news, and in between proved patient during a year of elevated monetary, fiscal, and geopolitical policy uncertainty.

The S&P 500 advanced approximately 16% in 2025, driven by 14% earnings growth.11 At year-end 2024, the index traded at 21.5 times 2025 estimated earnings. By year-end 2025, the multiple had expanded by just a half point to 22 times 2026E earnings.12 The gains were driven not by exuberance or valuation expansion, but by strong corporate performance.

While the Fed lowered rates three times, the cuts did not translate to a lower cost of capital.13 Almost immediately after the Fed started cutting rates 18 months ago, long rates started moving higher, and valuation is sensitive to long rather short rates. During that span, 10-year yields were up 25 basis points, while the 30-year gained 75 basis points.14 The weighted average cost of capital for S&P 500 constituents actually rose from 10.3% to 10.7% in 2025. Equities may have benefited from the expectation of lower rates, but it is hard to argue that equities rose because the cost of capital declined.

Meanwhile, economic conditions improved. GDP growth forecasts have been off the mark since 2023 with actuals consistently coming in better than expected. In 2025, GDP growth in the second and third quarters were roughly 50 basis points better than forecasted.15 Expectations for 2026 have followed a similar pattern with estimates now moving higher. At the start of 2026, the estimate for real GDP was 2.0%, and has since improved to 2.4%. Nominal GDP growth for the year ahead is now around 5.1%, meaningfully better than the 4.3% forecast at the May 2025 trough.16

Clues in Earnings Guidance

The company performance bar is high at the start of 2026. S&P 500 companies are forecast to grow revenues by 8% and earnings by 15%, with profit margins near record levels. Since 1992, S&P 500 companies have averaged 4% revenue growth and 9% EPS growth, making 2026 expectations an outlier.17 The risk embedded in current market pricing is that companies fail to meet or exceed estimates.

Earnings season gives companies an opportunity to refine guidance based on current data and potential scenarios for the year ahead. Interestingly, one-third of the way through this earnings season, most companies have raised their 2026 guidance. Of the 170 that had reported as of January 30, revenue estimates increased by 1%, EPS rose 0.5%, and 60% raised full-year guidance.18 That represents a solid setup.

Every S&P 500 sector has delivered positive earnings surprises thus far amid strong growth across most sectors. Tech and communication services profits have been strong, though industrials and materials lead the way.19 Financials, utilities, and energy have also delivered respectable year-over-year earnings performance. Importantly, growth and revisions aren’t limited to the Magnificent 7; a broader set of companies and industries are performing well. While staples, materials, healthcare, and discretionary have not, in aggregate, delivered positive profit growth thus far, all have posted upside surprises relative to prior forecasts and solid revenue growth.20

Bullish revisions to guidance are something of a historical anomaly. Since 1991, actual corporate earnings have typically come in 6% lower than the forecast to start the year, as investors begin with rosy expectations that are later revised to reflect economic reality.21 There is, however, a notable break in the data. Prior to 2015, the average deviation between forecast and actual S&P 500 earnings was 9% lower. Over the past decade, this relationship has changed, with actual earnings averaging 2% above expectations set 12 months earlier.22 The current revisions are therefore in line with more recent experience and suggest that actual earnings could still surprise to the upside.

Early January revisions are a positive sign for the year ahead. Historically, in years when S&P 500 earnings estimates are raised in January, full-year estimates also tend to move higher. Excluding the recessionary periods of 1991–1993 and 2001, as well as the post-pandemic economic snapback in 2020, there is a strong statistical correlation between January revisions and full-year performance.23 A 1% increase to January forecasts has historically translated into full-year earnings coming in about 2% above expectations, suggesting there may still be room for upside surprises despite already lofty expectations for 2026.

Market Rotation and Signs of Economic Broadening

The question we get a lot: when will the market broaden out? Mega-cap dominance, led by tech and communication services, has concentrated cap weights; however, a real economic broadening may be driving some of the market moves in early 2026.

While the primary equity indexes started 2026 higher, a rotation began in late January and carried into February.24 Former market darlings in software and data services fell, while commodities and energy outperformed. The contrast has been stark: the top-performing Bloomberg industry, commodity chemicals, is outperforming the weakest, application software, by 60% year-to-date as of February 4.25

S&P 500 factor performance tells a similar story. Liquidity, fundamentals, and size led the market higher in 2025, while the leading factors in January 2026 were momentum, liquidity, and dividend yield.26 Growth, quality, and size lagged. This rotation could be a knee-jerk reaction or a sign that other parts of the economy may be well positioned to improve productivity, leading to broader participation.

Evidence of broader economic participation is already working into expectations. From 2023 to 2025, the Mag 7 delivered nearly 25% annualized earnings growth, compared with less than 5% for the other 493 companies.27 That gap is forecast to narrow significantly over the next two years, with Mag 7 EPS growth slowing to 13% and the rest of the index accelerating to 11%.

While we have long expected productivity gains to emerge beyond big tech, fundamentals are still likely to win out, and the fundamentals for tech and growth equities remain strong. For example, the S&P 500 Software & Services Index is priced at 23.4 times 2026E earnings, supported by revenue and EPS growth of 19% and profit margins of 30%.28 That valuation still looks attractive relative to S&P 500 revenue growth of 8% and profit margins of 15% at 22 times 2026E earnings.

Rather than tossing the baby out with the bathwater, fundamentals can help navigate a prolonged shift in economic participation. Companies tied to the AI ecosystem are still likely to generate strong cash flow, but the onset of the Automation Age also likely creates opportunities in electrification, commodities, and industrials like infrastructure and defense.

At this stage of the AI buildout, efficiency is an operative word. The major AI players making big investments face a prove-it moment when monetization becomes critical, though much of this pressure is embedded in expectations. Over the next two years, earnings per dollar of capital expenditure for the Mag 7 is expected to decline but rise for the other 493 companies.29 By 2027, these companies are forecast to be roughly twice as efficient as the Mag 7 at converting corporate investment into earnings.

The efficiency angle also appears in expectations for profit margin improvement across sectors over the next two years.30 The best of both worlds may be achieved by owning the AI infrastructure and service providers alongside companies finding efficiency gains. The goal is to benefit from growth and profitability today while participating in the ongoing productivity and profitability shifts likely to emerge in the years ahead.

Footnotes

  1. Global X analysis with information derived from: Bloomberg L.P. (n.d.) [Data set]. Retrieved on January 30, 2026.
  2. McKinsey & Company. (2025, November 5). The state of AI in 2025: Agents, innovation, and transformation. The state of AI in 2025: Agents, innovation, and transformation
  3. Global X analysis with information derived from: Bloomberg L.P. (n.d.) [Data set]. Retrieved on January 30, 2026.
  4. Ibid.
  5. Helfstein, S. (2026, February 3). Hearing Warsh: Growth Along the Watchtower. Global X. Global X ETFs
  6. Federal Reserve. (2010, November 2-3). Meeting of the Federal Open Market Committee. https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/files/FOMC20101103meeting.pdf.
  7. Ibid.
  8. Global X analysis with information derived from: Bloomberg L.P. (n.d.) [Data set]. Retrieved on January 30, 2026.
  9. Ibid.
  10. Ibid.
  11. Ibid.
  12. Ibid.
  13. Ibid.
  14. Ibid.
  15. Ibid.
  16. Ibid.
  17. Ibid.
  18. Ibid.
  19. Ibid.
  20. Ibid.
  21. Ibid.
  22. Ibid.
  23. Ibid.
  24. Ibid.
  25. Global X analysis with information derived from: Bloomberg L.P. (n.d.) [Data set]. Retrieved on February 4, 2026.
  26. Global X analysis with information derived from: Bloomberg L.P. (n.d.) [Data set]. Retrieved on January 30, 2026.
  27. Ibid.
  28. Ibid.
  29. Ibid.
  30. Ibid.

Information provided by Global X Management Company LLC.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal. Diversification does not ensure a profit nor guarantee against a loss.

This material represents an assessment of the market environment at a specific point in time and is not intended to be a forecast of future events, or a guarantee of future results. This information is not intended to be individual or personalized investment or tax advice and should not be used for trading purposes. Please consult a financial advisor or tax professional for more information regarding your investment and/or tax situation.

Original Post

data.title: U.K. GDP Underwhelms With 0.1% Growth In Q4, FTSE 100 Slips And GBP/USD Advances
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

Preliminary data from the ONS indicates that the UK economy expanded by 0.1% in the final quarter of 2025, maintaining the same growth rate as the third quarter but falling just short of the 0.2% forecast by analysts.

This sluggish performance was marked by a significant divergence across sectors. The production industry provided a strong tailwind, surging 1.2% to recover from a previous decline, with manufacturing specifically rising 0.9% as automotive production stabilized following a major cyber disruption in August.

However, these gains were largely neutralized by a flatlining services sector, where business-facing activities stalled and consumer-facing services grew by a marginal 0.2%.

Furthermore, the construction sector acted as a significant drag on the economy, contracting by 2.1% and reversing its modest growth from the prior period.

The initial market reaction

Markets seemed to shrug off the lackluster data, with both GBP/USD and the FTSE 100 on the offensive this morning.

The FTSE 100 has since fallen around 50 points from the fresh all-time highs, while GBP/USD eyes a move higher in the European session.

GBP/USD movement may be driven in part by the US dollar and its performance today ahead of the CPI data tomorrow.

The big picture

When examining the broader economic landscape, the UK's GDP grew by 1.0% in the final quarter of 2025 compared to the same period in 2024. This steady annual performance was characterized by balanced contributions across the board, with both the services and production sectors expanding by 1.0%, while construction saw a more modest increase of 0.2%.

By the end of the year, December 2025 specifically showed a 0.7% improvement in economic output over the previous December, signaling a consistent, albeit measured, upward trend.

Taking the full year into account, the UK economy expanded by 1.3% in 2025 relative to 2024. This growth was primarily fueled by a 1.4% rise in services and a robust 1.8% increase in construction output.

Notably, the production sector returned to positive territory with a 0.2% increase, marking its first year of annual growth since 2021. Overall, these figures suggest a year of gradual recovery and the breaking of a multi-year stagnation in industrial output.

Is the lack of service sector growth a concern?

The services sector remained stagnant in the final quarter of 2025, recording zero growth in the three months leading up to December compared to the previous quarter.

This flat performance marks a continued trend of inertia, as recent data revisions have erased previously reported gains; growth for the periods ending in both October and November was adjusted downward to 0.0%.

Consequently, this latest update confirms a third consecutive month of stagnation for the UK's dominant economic sector on a rolling three-month basis.

While "zero growth" sounds alarming for a sector that makes up roughly 80% of the UK economy, the situation has been attributed to a mix of structural concern and temporary "Budget indigestion."

It does remain a concern though with the UK so dependent on services, the economy cannot achieve meaningful growth if this sector is flat. In Q4, the overall GDP only managed a tiny 0.1% increase, and that was largely thanks to a rebound in manufacturing.

One of the factors playing a role is the November 2025 budget. The increase in National Insurance contributions for employers and the rise in the minimum wage caused many service-based firms to freeze hiring and investment as they recalculated their costs.

In conclusion

It is a significant concern for the government's growth targets, as it shows the economy is currently walking a tightrope. However, many analysts remain optimistic, as the early 2026 data suggests it may be a "stumble" rather than a "fall."

Original Post

data.title: Astera's Pause Before The Second Surge
data.description:
A good quarter by Astera Labs (ALAB) but the stock move was based on expectation rather than reality. The stock went down because the investors were looking for acceleration, not normalization. The unexpected CFO change adds a layer of complexity to the growth story. The guidance was a little weaker than expected due to mix effects and investment. Operating costs are increasing as they move into bigger opportunities.

My thesis in December was around Astera’s structural change to become a scale-up control point with the view that Scorpio X would deliver a breakout year in 2026 based on increasing content per rack and hyperscaler engagement. What’s changed is the timing and the expectations. The growth rate is slowing down, the margins are being reset and Scorpio X’s acceleration is back half weighted, making it a story about execution.

A Strong Quarter Meets a Nervous Market

The fourth quarter was one of those quarters where beating expectations doesn’t even require an excuse. Revenue was $270.6 million, up 17% sequentially and up 92% year over year. Revenue for the year came in at $852.5 million, up 115%. Non-GAAP gross margin was again high at 75.7%. Operating cash flows were $95.3 million, leaving the company with $1.19 billion in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities.

However, the stock move quickly shifted from the good quarter to the guidance. Revenue for the first quarter is expected to be $286 million to $297 million. This is still growth but only 6% to 10% sequentially. Gross margin is expected to be around 74%, while operating costs increase meaningfully. This is a near-term reset in the margin profile, and I can see why investors are nervous.

Where Protocol Wars Meet Platform Power

The call was dominated by questions about UALink, NVLink Fusion, Ethernet scale up, and ESUN. My interpretation is that Ethernet is optimized for scale out while PCI Express is optimized for low latency attachment and memory level workflows within the rack. Scale up is where the architecture becomes personal, and hyperscalers follow their software stack rather than a standard.

This is the reason I continue to focus on Astera’s core strength. Astera is not just providing a piece part. Astera is qualifying itself as the connectivity piece for memory centric designs and then leveraging that to go through multiple generations. If they are going to be important, they have the ability to design into that, but the opportunity today is around PCI Express, UALink, and custom scale up.

Scorpio X: The Real Acceleration Engine

Scorpio P is already in volume but Scorpio X is the product that sets the pace for the next acceleration. It has modest contributions in the first half of 2026, a transition to high volumes in the second half of 2026, and more ramping in 2027. That’s why the growth in the first quarter looks so benign compared to the double-digit growth in the previous quarters.

What makes Scorpio X interesting is not the size, it’s the positioning. Scale up fabrics have become more customized, and Astera is trying to leverage silicon while differentiating on software-defined features and system-level integration. When they get it right, they can serve more topologies without having to do a new tape out.

Another thing I’m looking at is the mix within Scorpio. The P series is scale out, the X series is scale up, where the dollars per rack can be richer. And so, if the X can ramp as they expect, they can grow the company even as Aries and Taurus normalize from the surge in 2025.

Winning on Both Sides of the AI Divide

Astera is doubling down on the opportunity with UALink as an open, high performance scale-up protocol, with public customer roadmaps calling out to 2027 ramp times. At the same time, Astera is calling out NVLink Fusion as an add-on opportunity, with Astera taking their native protocol and converting it to NVLink, attaching to hybrid racks on a one-to-one basis per accelerator. The revenue opportunity, according to management, has the potential to be similar to selling a switched fabric, as the attach rates can help offset the lower economics of the sale.

I like this positioning as it removes the risk of choosing the wrong ecosystem. In this industry with hyperscalers building their own custom accelerators and still deploying Nvidia-based solutions, Astera can be relevant to both, as long as they stay within the custom path and maintain their higher content.

Amazon’s Warrant: Trust & Accounting Noise

Astera filed their warrant agreement related to Amazon purchases, which can result in purchases of up to $6.5 billion related to smart fabric switch, signal conditioning and optical engine products. This is not an easy agreement to make, and it speaks volumes to the trust and confidence that Amazon has in Astera, as well as their desire to incent their partners over time.

The issue of course is accounting, as the company will incur a non-cash expense as tranches vest, which will effectively lower revenue and gross margins, with management calling out a two-point gross margin reduction starting in the second quarter. In other words, their margins will be lower just as this relationship is starting to prove itself.

A Valuation Premium That Demands Perfection

ALAB is a company that is trading at a valuation which assumes flawless execution. If we look at trailing numbers, the company is trading at over 40x sales and over 100x non-GAAP earnings, while the sector averages are in the mid-20s on the earnings side and low single digits on the sales side.

Even if we look at forward numbers ALAB trades at a forward Price/Sales of 22x and an even higher multiple on the forward Price/Earnings. The consensus estimate is that the company will earn around $2.54 per share in 2026 and $3.44 per share in 2027 which is clearly going to be a growth curve, but the current valuation already assumes a lot of that growth.

The company is at the center of the AI connectivity universe and has increasing content per rack, which is why the premium exists. The problem is that the valuation leaves no room for any issues with the ramp of the Scorpio X product, any issues with hyperscaler spend and any issues with margin volatility. The stock is not a value play at this point but a growth play with a long duration and assumes acceleration.

4 Risks the Market Can’t Ignore

The first risk is protocol drift. Should the scale-up architectures standardize along the path where Astera is not included, the ramps may not materialize as expected.

The second risk is timing. The Scorpio X has lengthy qualification cycles. Any slippage in the timeline may pressure the stock as it is valued for second-half acceleration. The third risk is competition. The retimers, cables, and switches have aggressive competitor activity and internal designs from large customers.

And lastly the fourth risk is optical timing risk. The 2028 expectations for co-packaged optics may cause sentiment to accelerate ahead of the revenue realization.

Bottom Line

I’m not worried about the one-quarter margin move but about remaining embedded in the custom rack solution. Scorpio X represents the second growth wave, and the Amazon warrant proves that there’s real pull from the customers, even if it’s a distortion in the gross margin picture in the short term. The 2027s ramping as planned makes the current slowdown appear to be a consolidation rather than a breakdown.

data.title: Grab: Valuation Compression Is Attractive As Company Sets Aggressive Targets
data.description:
2026 is proving to be an exceptionally tricky stock market to invest in. The Q4 earnings season has spurred even greater volatility, and investors appear ready to sell off stocks at the slightest provocation. Most of the growth stock space has seen sharp corrections of 20% or more, without much regard to their actual fundamental performance. While it's true that many had outsized valuations that were long overdue a correction, in many cases, the selloff has gone too far.

Grab (GRAB), in my view, represents a perfect buy-the-dip opportunity. Southeast Asia's version of Uber (UBER) continues to showcase tremendous bookings growth, coupled with the inherent economic strength of the region it serves. The company just reported strong Q4 results, and despite it, Grab stock fell ~5% post-earnings, bringing its losses since the start of January to more than 20% alone.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Grab in November, when the stock was trading in the mid-$5s. Since then, I fully acknowledge that Grab has been a big disappointment in my portfolio. That said, the position fulfills a key objective that I have for the year: to increase my exposure to international stocks, especially those operating in attractive emerging markets (and in Grab's case, with quite low geopolitical and headline risk). We also like the company's march toward substantial growth and profitability gains, and I'm more than comfortable holding onto my position and reiterating my "Buy" rating at current levels.

One of the key anchors for my renewed optimism in Grab is the company's aggressive 2028 targets. For FY26 specifically, the company is still targeting 20%-22% y/y growth, basically representing no deceleration at all from its current growth pace. It's also expecting meaningful margin expansion next year, with adjusted EBITDA growth expected to double revenue growth.

The company, meanwhile, has set medium-term targets for FY28. Through that year, the company is still guiding to a sustained pace of ~20% growth CAGR, with the intention of hitting $1.5 billion in adjusted EBITDA by FY28 - with 80% FCF conversion. That represents roughly doubling versus adjusted EBITDA expectations in FY26 and tripling versus FY25.

None of these targets suggest Grab's underlying business momentum is at risk of a slowdown. And yet, while Grab isn't quite a bargain stock yet, its recent share price losses have made its stock far more appealing, especially in the context of robust targets.

At post-earnings share prices hovering around $4, Grab trades at a market cap of $17.29 billion. Netting off the $3.43 billion of cash and $2.05 billion of debt on Grab's most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $15.91 billion.

This positions Grab's valuation at 22.4x EV/FY26 adjusted EBITDA. If we take Grab's plan to hit $1.5 billion in adjusted EBITDA by FY28 at face value, then adjusted EBITDA multiples against longer-term targets cut roughly in half. In my view, I find this to be more than a fair price to pay for Grab's aggressive growth rates.

With Grab's recent declines due more to overarching market sentiment than to actual company performance, I'd happily take the opportunity to buy the dip here.

Q4 Download

Let's now go through Grab's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The chart below showcases the company's trends in its largest segment, the Deliveries business:

As a reminder, unlike U.S. rival Grab, which has a roughly even split between rideshare and delivery in terms of contribution to overall company financials, Grab's Deliveries business predominates, generating roughly two-thirds of the company's overall bookings. GMV in this segment rose 21% y/y to $3.90 billion, barely at all decelerating from 22% constant-currency growth in Q3. Meanwhile, we note in particular that this segment has always been slower to ramp up in terms of profitability, in part because the company spends heavily on promotions to drive bookings, especially in competitive and critical markets like Indonesia, where Grab has fierce competition against Gojek. Still, we note the fact that Deliveries adjusted EBITDA grew at a 47% y/y pace to $84 million, reflecting a 2.2% margin on GMV, rising 40bps y/y.

Meanwhile, the Mobility segment achieved 19% y/y constant-currency growth to $2.17 billion in GMV.

We note that the Mobility segment's pace of growth actually accelerated 2 points y/y versus 17% y/y growth in Q3. Again, this doesn't indicate at all that Grab is facing any macro-related issues.

Meanwhile, the company's financial services/loan portfolio continued to grow. The total outstanding loans grew 120% y/y to $1.18 billion. And though not profitable yet, we note that adjusted EBITDA margins in this segment improved 11 points y/y to -25.6%:

From an overall company perspective, we think the company's achievement of 20% GMV growth is consistent with its aggressive FY28 targets. Total company adjusted EBITDA of $148 million also grew 54% y/y, far outpacing revenue growth and representing meaningful margin expansion, which helps us to take comfort in the fact that Grab isn't locked into major price wars with any of its competitors to maintain market share.

Risks and Key Takeaways

In this year's tough market, investors should take comfort in investing in solid, market-leading businesses at good prices, and Grab's declining valuation makes for a de-risked entry point in the stock.

Of course, we should take care to recognize the fundamental risks the company faces. A growing loan portfolio means a higher risk of defaults, especially if macro shocks (or tariffs) hit Southeast Asia disproportionately. Competitors like Gojek could also step up their incentive spending, forcing Grab to do the same.

That said, I see more positives than risks for Grab, especially as the company eyes a target of tripling its adjusted EBITDA profitability (relative to FY25) within a three-year timeframe, marking an aggressive commitment to profit expansion. Stay long here.

data.title: Enovis: Valuation Is Too Low, But Needs To Differentiate On Growth
data.description:
Smaller med-tech can be a value trap until and unless it distinguishes itself on growth; as a former sell-side med-tech analyst, I can tell you that there are many institutions that won’t even consider a smaller med-tech if it’s not growing at a double-digit rate (often 20%+). That’s an ongoing problem for Enovis (ENOV), as the market just doesn’t seem to care about this company so long as revenue growth remains stuck in the mid-single-digits and free cash flow generation is marginal at best.

Enovis shares have continued to lose ground, falling another 30% since my last update against a roughly 6% fall in the broader medical device space and single-digit to mid-teens declines for larger ortho rivals like Smith & Nephew (SNN), Stryker (SYK), and Zimmer Biomet (ZBH).

Management guidance for potentially low double-digit growth in the Recon business in 2026 is positive, but overall mid-single-digit growth guidance isn’t moving the needle on estimates or sentiment. Enovis looks cheap by virtually every metric I use, but that has been the case for some time now, and a lack of differentiated growth remains a major headwind for the shares.

Q4 Should Be Largely De-Risked Now

Between guidance given with third quarter earnings and updated guidance at a major sell-side conference in January, I would say the fourth quarter results (due to be reported on Feb. 26) are largely de-risked, and if there are any major negative surprises, the market reaction will be brutal.

Management has been clear about fewer selling days in the quarter, and the latest revision to guidance (to $2.245 billion from $2.26 billion at the midpoint) pretty much dials in Q4 revenue to $567 million to $577 million, though the Street seems to be holding on to a little optimism for a beat, as the average estimate currently sits at $582 million.

With pharmacy strikes likely creating a little noise in OUS results, I wouldn’t think this would be the quarter for a substantial beat, but then I would note that results from larger orthos have generally been stronger than expected this quarter, so that would argue for some upside in Recon. I’d also hope to see some leverage/momentum from Nebula Stem and Surgical Impactor in the hip business.

Looking for Acceleration

Looking back at third-quarter results, Enovis didn’t really distinguish itself to the extent it needs to for the shares to really work.

The Recon business was up 9% overall, with global major joints up 7% versus around 5.8% growth for the four majors. Underlying results were a bit more mixed, with capital headwinds pushing U.S. hips/knees down to 1% contraction. Looking through the capital headwinds, like-for-like joint revenue was up “mid-single-digits” versus a little over 5% growth for the sector as a whole. Overseas sales were stronger (up 7%) and compare more favorably, but the market doesn’t value this as highly.

I’m not expecting much from the Prevention & Recovery business. This has generally been a 3%-4% grower, a bit better than the underlying market, but I don’t see any real catalysts here for acceleration. Medicare coverage for its continuous motion cold therapy device is nice, but likely not enough to really change the pace of revenue growth, and management’s preliminary guidance of low-to-mid-single-digit growth reflects that.

Recon is perhaps a different story. Management’s initial guide was for high single-digit to low double-digit growth here for 2026, but the Street has gotten more wary of guidance here given past disappointments.

On the positive side, the hip and knee markets both seem healthy going into 2026. While factors like GLP-1 drug usage could impact long-term patient funnels, that’s much too far in the future to change the near-term outlook. What is more relevant to me is that the company is starting to introduce products from its European Mathys business (which it acquired around five years ago).

Mathys has a low-teens share in the European hip market, making it a much more relevant rival to the ortho majors than it is in the United States. Porting these products over seems like a no-brainer, but it takes time to go through the regulatory processes. For 2026, there should finally be some material positive developments with the introduction of the Optimys stem late in the year. This hip has shown noticeably lower revision rates than competing hips and could be a share-gainer for Enovis in the U.S. business over time.

Another potential driver is the upcoming launch of the new ARVIS Ultra. Management has taken its time with this new iteration and expects to launch in 1H’26. This is a compact augmented reality system that is meant to provide many of the benefits of robotic systems (help with alignment and guiding cuts) in a smaller, cheaper form factor. While Enovis has reported strong feedback from its user base, I am skeptical that it’s going to move the needle much against Stryker and its MAKO system in major joints—at this point, I think alternative navigation approaches like ARVIS and Smith & Nephew’s Cori just aren’t enough to do much more than keep existing users happy.

The extremities business is another key potential driver of better growth. The shoulder business continues to outperform, with the ARG gaining share in the market. Further product innovation, including products from the Lima business in Europe, should help preserve double-digit growth here, and I think the shoulder business is an area where the new ARVIS system could be more valuable and differentiating, and Enovis will be showcasing the system at the upcoming AAOS meeting in early March.

The Outlook

Assuming no major surprises in Q4, Enovis did a little better than I expected in 2025, and I haven’t changed my estimates all that much. I’m expecting 4%-5% growth in 2026 and around 5% growth over the next three years, with the Prevention and Recovery business continuing to weigh on the overall growth rate. Longer term, I’m still looking for around 5% long-term annualized revenue growth.

For margins, I expect the EBITDA margin to improve to 18% next year, with some upside if there’s a positive resolution on tariffs. Although management has reiterated its commitment to driving EBITDA margins above 20%, largely through improving gross margin and SG&A efficiency, I’m skeptical they’ll get there anytime soon, and I’m sticking with 19% EBITDA margin expectations in five years.

This, in turn, should support low double-digit FCF margins, but it remains to be seen if management can rein in relatively high capex spending, and there are always going to be elevated working capital requirements to support the Recon business.

Valuation continues to be frustrating. I can argue for double-digit long-term annualized return potential based on discounted cash flow. Likewise, even if I give the P&R business a 1x revenue multiple (arguably unfair given mid-teens EBITDA margins and strong cash generation), a blended growth/margin-based EV/revenue of 2x would support a fair value in the $50s. I can also get to a mid-$30s fair value with a forward P/E ratio of 10x, which is quite low for a growing med-tech.

The Bottom Line

I can make plenty of arguments as to why Enovis should be trading higher than it is, but market sentiment is what it is. The Street doesn’t like slow-growing med-tech businesses (even if they’re profitable and cash-generative), and I can understand the lack of confidence in Enovis’s ability to gain share long-term in the hip and knee markets. That leaves a lot riding on the extremities business, and I believe investors also want to see more movement on margin improvement.

What to do with the shares? I’m tempted to go to “Neutral” given that I don’t think the Recon business is likely to accelerate enough to resolve the growth issues, and I don’t know what will change the Street’s mind otherwise. On the other hand, expectations are so low now, and with past acquisitions like Lima and Mathys starting to contribute, there could be more upside here than the Street acknowledges. I maintain my "Buy" rating.

data.title: Rates Spark: Domestic Drivers More Important Than U.S. Spillovers
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker, Senior European Rates Strategist

GBP and EUR rates doing their own thing despite big UST moves

US payrolls pushed US Treasury yields significantly higher, but the spillovers to EUR and GBP rates were remarkably muted. Gilt and Bund yields rose initially on the data release but were also quick to retrace that increase and actually ended the day lower than the open. We have observed the correlation between USD rates and other markets decline since the presidential elections in 2024, but rarely do we see days when rates move in opposite directions.

Gilt yields are usually a lot more sensitive to US rate moves – but in the UK, domestic stories are growing more dominant. A political risk premium is likely to continue driving Gilts in the foreseeable future as Labour’s poor polling calls for a change in direction. But sterling markets are also seeing an increasingly high chance of another Bank of England cut in March, even though the latest US payrolls data has pushed the next Federal Reserve cut to June or July. This morning's weak monthly UK GDP data from December supports a more dovish turn from the Bank of England. The diverging narratives between the US and UK may therefore allow Gilt yields to ease lower despite the upward pressure on UST yields.

More joint EU issuance unlikely to be decided today

Today, we have EU leaders meeting in Brussels to further discuss, among other things, defence with NATO representatives. We'll be listening for hints about potential future joint debt issuance. Yesterday, the EU parliament did vote in favour of a €90bn loan to Ukraine, which would be funded jointly. Having said that, at this stage we doubt more will be promised despite calls from, for example, French President Emmanuel Macron earlier this week to increase joint debt. Instead, this discussion is probably more important from a structural perspective on the EU's role in broader economic investments. So far, not everyone is enthusiastic, however, and we might find some opposition from countries like Germany.

Meanwhile, investors continue to like EU bonds, with another heavily oversubscribed order book for the dual tranche EU deal on Tuesday. Spreads for EU bonds in the 10y area are just below 30bp, placing them wider than Germany but also notably tighter than France, for instance. The EU has an AAA rating, which makes the bonds part of a fairly exclusive selection of highest-quality assets when assessed at a global level. Together with Germany, the EU is one of the largest issuers globally for AAA-rated debt.

Thursday’s events and market views

After yesterday’s big upside surprise in US payroll numbers, markets will be watching the weekly jobless claims numbers for more timely signs of labour market strength. The US will also publish existing home sales data for January, although this is usually not a market mover. In terms of speakers, we have the European Central Bank's Joachim Nagel due to speak on monetary policy.

In terms of supply, we have Italy with a 3Y, 6Y and 7Y BTP auction totalling €6.25bn. From the US, we have a 30Y Bond auction for $25bn, which follows on a weaker 10y sale that helped push yields higher overnight.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Tri Ponte Homes: Q4 May Represent A Bottom, But Valuations Still Look Concerning
data.description:
A Move-Up Ready Premium Homebuilder That Has Underperformed Its Peers

Tri Ponte Homes (TPH), which has its roots in Southern California, but has since expanded to 11 other states and the District of Columbia (see image below, which showcases geographic exposure across the Western, Central, and Eastern markets), over the last 16 years (it was established towards the end of the GFC and homebuilding crisis of 2009), is currently in the business of selling single-family attached and detached homes.

Unlike most of the competing homebuilders, TPH distinguishes itself by orienting its business mix around premium lifestyle move-up ready homes (the average sales price of its current backlog is $745K to $781K) rather than entry-level, high-volume homes (typically $190K- $250K).

Notwithstanding its position in the US homebuilding market, over the past year, the TPH’s stock has failed to make any meaningful headway, rising by less than 4%. This is quite a meaningful bout of underperformance by this mid-cap stock (TPH’s current market cap works out to around $3B) when you consider that other peers from the Russell 2000 have generated returns that are over 5x more! In addition to that, TPH has also not managed to generate one-fourth the returns that other homebuilders have managed over the past year (~15%).

Clearly TPH needs to get its act together, and in two weeks’ time, it could encounter an ideal catalyst in the shape of its Q4 earnings that will be released pre-market on the 25th of February.

So, what can investors expect from this event, and is TPH worth pursuing ahead of its earnings?

What Are the Key Subplots to Watch Out for at TPH’s Upcoming Q4 Earnings Event?

Firstly, when it comes to the earnings headlines, we don’t see why investors should feel overly anxious, as TPH has quite a pristine 4-year track record of relentlessly beating market estimates both on the topline and bottom-line every single quarter. So, based on history, we know that there’s a high probability of TPH beating expectations, but what sort of cadence could be taken well by the market?

Well, we feel a topline beat of anything over 9%, and an earnings beat of anything over 24%, will likely be appreciated by the market, as this is typically what TPH has been accustomed to facilitating over the last 16 quarters.

The prospect of a revenue beat has been lifted even further, as consensus has turned quite conservative over the last 3 months, curtailing their revenue expectations by almost 8% to $917.86M (in contrast, EPS adjustments for Q4 over the last 3 months have been upgraded to $0.79, but by a much lower threshold of less than 2%)!

TPH is also expected to deliver a higher number of homes in Q4 (close to 1300), as opposed to what was seen in Q3 (1217 homes), but investors should still be aware that even a ballpark figure of 1300 deliveries would still imply a hefty YoY decline of over 25%, which is worse than the 22% decline seen in Q3.

It’s been well documented that the macro and consumer environment hasn’t been conducive enough for the US homebuilders to grow their business, and TPH is no exception to this trend, even if it is not overly exposed to the most economically weak segment of the market (entry-level buyers) like other mid-cap homebuilders. TPH’s topline has been contracting annually for four straight quarters (while the degree of contraction has only been expanding sequentially all through 9M-25)

Note that in Q4, the pace of revenue decline is expected to come in at a similar threshold to Q3 (a decline of over 25% YoY), and while these annual declines will likely persist for two more quarters, it looks like Q4 could represent a bottom of sorts.

Nonetheless, we wouldn’t advise investors to jump the gun, and expect a meaningful uplift in TPH’s Q1-26 guidance, as broader industry metrics in early 2026 still point to ongoing weakness in the homebuilding markets. Take note of the NAHB Housing Market Index, which last month slumped yet again to 4-month lows. Even the expectations for single family housing sales over the next 6 months have turned for the worse in January.

What we also find troubling to note is that the traffic of prospective buyers still continues to remain at a low level compared to a year ago. In a survey conducted by NAHB last month, the risk of buyers waiting for better prices has emerged as the biggest concern for 2026.

Do consider that TPH has already been raising the level of incentives it sees on its deliveries for 5 straight quarters (it was over 8% of total deliveries in Q3, with one-third of this consisting of financing incentives like mortgage rate buydowns alone), and doubling down on incentives even further to attract buyers won’t reflect well on TPH, which likes to be seen as a premium play in the housing market.

One of the plus points of Q4 will be the seasonal pickup in free cash flow (traditionally, Q2 and Q3 tend to see a spike in working capital commitments, particularly on the inventory front), which has been negative all through 2025 (that wasn’t the case last year when TPH had already seen $335.7M of positive operating cash).

Note that inventory turns are currently trending lower than the historical average of 1.24x, offering some upward scope of normalization

Considering how tough the first 9 months have been, even if we see a solid FCF performance in Q4, overall free cash flow in annual terms (relative to FY24) is expected to be much lower, and this will reflect poorly on a stock that has traditionally offered solid enough FCF yields within the double-digit threshold (more on this later). Nonetheless, the likely sequential improvement in FCF also means that TPH should have enough firepower to wrap up its $300M share buyback plan that was due to expire by FY25 (by the end of 9M-25, TPH had the authorization to spend close to $74M in Q4 to hit this target).

With the buyback plan having expired in FY25, it will be intriguing to see if TPH management announces a fresh buyback plan during the Q4 results, as the TPH stock’s valuations are not exactly cheap (more on this later), while leverage levels too have stepped up through the year (homebuilding debt to capital has grown from 21.6% to 25.1%).

Is TPH Stock A Good Buy Before Its Q4 Earnings?

No, we don’t believe TPH would make for a rewarding buy at this juncture.

Firstly, consider that TPH’s valuations look quite unappealing. Investors should note that even though TPH hasn’t witnessed any meaningful price appreciation, its forward valuations have still expanded a great deal (it is now priced at an earnings multiple of 16x, which is a whopping 66% more than its historical rolling average of less than 10x), making it come across as one of the costliest homebuilders around.

To better explain our point, we can also compare TPH to other mid-cap homebuilders, such as M/I Homes (MHO), Green Brick Partners (GRBK), Taylor Morrison Home Corp (TMHC), Champion Homes (SKY), Century Communities (CCS), and KB Homes (KBH). The average multiple of these mid-cap homebuilding alternatives works out to only around 14x, and it could potentially be less than 13x, if not for SKY’s multiple, which is something of an outlier. However, one could argue that SKY at least deserves to trade at that premium multiple, as it is the only homebuilder from this lot that is set to witness annualized positive earnings growth for 2026.

It is difficult to reconcile TPH being the second most expensive mid-cap homebuilder at 16x, when it is poised to witness a pronounced contraction in earnings of -20% YoY (only KBH, which is set to witness an annualized contraction of -32% for 2026 is expected to fare worse), which is around 700bps lower than the pace at which these homebuilders are poised to contract on average (-13%).

From another lens, investors may also want to consider that TPH is currently not generating ample enough free cash flow relative to its market-cap, at least by historical standards. On a trailing twelve-month basis, TPH’s annualized FCF is only 40% of what it was over a year ago, and the lack of meaningful progress on the market-cap also means that TPH’s current FCF yield is almost 550bps lower than what the stock has traditionally yielded (less than 14% over the past 5 years).

Then, also take note of the developments of TPH’s weekly charts, where the risk-reward for a long position isn’t the most optimal. After bouncing off its support in early January (the black line), the stock has already rebounded by over 16%, and is currently on the cusp of breaching price levels that had previously worked as a resistance (highlighted in pink), in late 2024, and September 2025. Meanwhile, you also have a scenario now where the price is now trading above its upper Bollinger Band, which implies some overextension to the upside, given that the band is two standard deviations higher than the 20-week rolling average.

data.title: Have Tech Stocks Hit A Reset Moment?
data.description:
While tech firms may benefit from a longer-term “renaissance” in AI, TD Wealth’s Chief Wealth Strategist Brad Simpson says the recent selloff may be a “reset moment” for the sector.

Transcript

Anthony Okolie: While many of the high-flying tech stocks have come under pressure recently, our featured guest today says that investors need to be thinking about the longer-term renaissance that's happening in the sector. Joining us now with more is Brad Simpson, Chief Wealth Strategist with TD Wealth.

And Brad, welcome to the show.

Brad Simpson: It's great to be here.

Anthony Okolie: Alright. So, for people who haven't seen it yet, what's the big theme of your latest portfolio strategy quarterly? And what inspired that title?

Brad Simpson: Yeah, it's a big title, isn't it? "The Brand New Renaissance." I think the reality is, we wanted to make a publication that, on first blush, it seems like a lot of hyperbole. We are of this belief that-- and I think that we are in the middle of the second Renaissance right now. And the first one was 500 years ago, and we're in the middle of a new one. And I don't think that's an overstatement.

And so what inspired us was, on one hand, was that we really wanted to map that out and what does that mean-- take it and go back a little bit and actually look at the original Renaissance and what were some of the implications of it, and then compare it to today.

But also to hit home that this is happening at a massive rate, like a 10 times rate. The Renaissance unrolled over a series over a couple hundred years. We're seeing this unfold rapidly, and it's changing not just in the things that we're seeing around us, but how we think about our cognition and the execution of that. And so, on the one hand, we wanted to be clear on that.

On the other hand, which I think the dichotomy comes in, we also said that-- we're also moving into where you have to go beyond, in 2026, words and narrative. And it's going to have to-- '26 is going to start to be about delivery, execution, productivity, thinking about how are we actually going to move at scale into an environment that looks like this. And I think that really was the motivation for it. And then I think the important part as an investor is to really understand that.

Anthony Okolie: Alright. Let's talk a little bit about some of the sectors as we start 2026. Technology, of course, has been in the headlines. And we've seen a pullback in tech valuations, particularly software. How are you thinking about that risk looking ahead?

Brad Simpson: Yeah. Look, sometimes when you publish something and then you kind of-- and then sometimes you get lucky. And I think in this instance we did, because really, if you look at the events over the last week was a case study for what we're talking about. And so, we look out and Anthropic (ANTHRO) comes out and says that, well, they have a new set-- they have a tool set for software, for corporations, you might not necessarily have to go out and purchase the software. Instead, you can use Anthropic and you can use their tool set to either develop your own or to interlink and start to build the work with the tools that you already have.

So now if we go back, and what we're looking at here is if we go back into the fall of last year, we've already seen the beginning of a big correction in software prices. And that's what we're looking on the left-hand side here, seeing software heading down from its massive outperformance to working its way over to the S&P 500. The other side we're looking at is, looking at their valuations on an earnings basis. And we're going from 23-- going from around 30 times earnings to getting around a zone of market valuations in it. That's a big change.

And so, you've got to step back a little bit and you have to look at this and go, "Well, why is this happening?" And so, what we wrote about in "The Brand New Renaissance" is that what's going to start happening is that, from this narrative, you're going to start to see the change. And so, one of the things that we pointed out, if you look at the fourth quarter and you start looking at public companies-- public companies on the high end of the spectrum of using AI, what we started to see is real productivity gains.

And those real productivity gains-- it's early stages and we have a long ways to go, but you're starting to see that happen. And so, when we look at this and the market steps back and goes, well, boy, if we think about what an amazing impact software companies had and how much money people have made owning software companies, then you'd have to look at this and go, well, what's this threat, and what does that look like?

And so if you tied a little bit into-- when we were coming in, in one of-- you were saying that Thomson Reuters has had a nice bump. So, what the market said last week is a perfect example of the concern about software. At the end of the day-- and this is, I think, probably really important to a lot of Canadians who are watching, because Thomson is a kind of a core holding for a lot of Canadian investors.

And ultimately, Thomson is an aggregator of information and data. It's aggregating and taking that information that is out there, and then it's reforming it and sending it back out again. You'll often see on that chart we're looking at there, it says FactSet as one of our sources for doing this. If you go a year ago, FactSet was trading at 30 times earnings. It's trading about 12 to 15 times earnings now. It's the same thing-- it's aggregating that data, and it's sending that data out.

And so, that's the market looking at-- and that's where I'm saying where the rubber hits the road. That's the market looking at it and saying, wait a minute here. If AI has the ability to be able to aggregate that data, and I'm in a large financial organization, that-- boy, they might be inclined to be able to use that as a tool and may not necessarily be so inclined to be able to do that.

In a nutshell, what we saw is that what I think was one of the main parts of what has driven a lot of our corporations. I mean, how much time do you-- you spend an enormous amount of time on using software. I spend that. From the FactSet to, I have a lot of employees, I use Workday. What does Workday really do? Workday just aggregates data and sends it back out again. All of this is at the heart of a corporation. And if you start thinking, OK, what does that mean? You'll see some pretty dramatic repricing go on. And we think we're just-- get used to it, because we're going to see more and more and more of this.

Now, so I'm clear on this is not saying the end is nigh for software. We published today a follow-up piece that we called "AI Eats the Software World?"-- with a question mark. And I think what we want to do is we want to step back a little bit and go-- because I think there's a lot of learning, and there's a lot of learning for investors here.

One of the things that you and I have spoken of a lot about, and one of the real popular things for investors to talk about, is beta. So, looking at just go buy an index and get the return. Alpha-- trying to produce a rate of return where you have an analyst and a portfolio manager who's making a difference. What's going to happen, we think moving forward, is that you'll start to assess, like software companies, and look and go, what are the ones that it's easy for AI to be able to take and eat their lunch, if you will? And where are the areas where that's going to be more difficult?

So you'll get a big correction like this one where it just cuts everybody off at the knees. But over time what will happen, there'll be winners and losers in this exchange. And we think in active management, that's also where your opportunities are going to be.

And we think that-- why I'm digging into this so much is that I really want investors to understand what I like about this so much is this is, in real time, what we're going to be seeing we think for the rest of 2026, and what we think we're going to see in the coming quarters, in the coming years. And it's a nice, tight way of going, wow, OK-- and the learnings that we have from this, let's think about how it's going to affect other ways we're going to make decisions.

Original Post

data.title: Wallenius Wilhelmsen: Things Looking Up In 2026
data.description:
Wallenius Wilhelmsen ASA (WAWIF) had a year marred by issues around general trade flows, as we discussed in our previous coverage. There are some bright spots as we look towards 2026, and it's consistent with the improved EBITDA outlook for 2026 compared to the annualised figures from Q4. Rates started to improve after Q4 despite considerable vessel deliveries. They will lap issues from the government shutdown in the US in 2026. Mercosur-EU trade deal could also improve flows and backhauling irregularities in the EU-Asia route. Directionally, 2026 could be a lot better. An important factor will be a permanent suspension of the port fees between the US and China, which was a risk factor that weighed on the stock until the fee truce in November 2025. Another year of decent EBITDA on a low-multiple shipping stock is a good thing for the stock. It's cheaper than Höegh Autoliners ASA (HOEGF), too, on an EV/EBITDA forward basis (3.7x against 4.64x).

Results

The rates started to improve after the close of Q4. But otherwise lower rates weighed on the overall results. This was despite volume growth, particularly in the Asia-EU routes.

This reflected in the overall negative EBITDA performance QoQ and YoY for Q4.

A couple of standout mentions in terms of segment performance were the really weak showing from the government services segment. Other than rate hits, shipping services saw their margins hit by a skew of trade towards volumes coming out of Europe (the backhaul), where rates are generally less. This reduced margin on the EU-Asia route, which should otherwise be a profit driver, was unique to the quarter. In general, there is more coming out of Asia, not Europe.

This was directly related to staffing issues under the US government shutdown, which simply resulted in no calls coming in for WW services in that period. This is normalising, and there is nothing impaired about that end market.

For logistics, the effect was partially the MIRRAT sale, done at around a 2x EV/EBITDA multiple when annualising the MIRRAT EBITDA contribution in the first 120 days of the year (not a particularly good multiple, lower than the company's comprehensive EV/EBITDA), but also due to underlying factors. In particular, they do terminals in the East Coast US and also in Europe. The weaker EU-NA flows and the weaker NA-Asia flows affected the business.

Bottom Line

The forecast is between $1.65-1.75 million for 2026, which signals a considerable improvement from Q4 run-rates. On the normalised governmental segment, passing the backhaul skew, general rate increases post Q4, and also possibly higher flow rates between Mercosur nations and the EU, with a new trade integration event, support the outlook.

The primary risks are the end of the US-China port fee truce without a positive resolution, which would expire in November 2026, and also more hits to trade flows, for example the threatened tariffs against South Korea (major car exporter), possibly linked to SK government pressure on a US company considered discriminatory by the US, would threaten the currently positive incremental view. WW is also cheaper than some other Norwegian RoRo comps. Another solid year expected in the books is always good news for a company that trades in terms of multiples in the low single-digits - good payback rates.
